skill.name: copilot-sdk
skill.description: Build applications powered by GitHub Copilot using the Copilot SDK across Node.js, Python, Go, and .NET. The skill explains sessions, streaming, custom tools, hooks, MCP servers, BYOK providers, persistence, and custom agents.
skill.summary: Build GitHub Copilot SDK integrations with sessions, tools, hooks, MCP servers, and BYOK providers.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows they are mostly Markdown backticks, code fences, type tables, and SDK examples rather than executable skill code. The skill is not malicious and no prompt injection was found, but it documents local commands, MCP server execution, network endpoints, and environment-based credentials, so publication should include a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Local MCP Server Command Example Requires Review
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for elevated operational risk. The skill documents a local MCP server launched with npx and filesystem access to /allowed/path. This is legitimate SDK guidance, but users must constrain paths, packages, and tools before production use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly configure a local command and filesystem MCP server. The example appears instructional, but the pattern can expose local files if copied with broad paths or tools.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling Examples Need Secret Controls
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for handling sensitive credentials. The skill explains GitHub token lookup and BYOK API key use through environment variables. This is expected for SDK authentication, but it involves secrets and should be paired with secret-management guidance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly reference GitHub token environment variables and a provider API key. The usage is legitimate and not exfiltrating data, but it is still sensitive credential handling.

medium_findings.title: Broad Permission Handler Example Could Be Misused
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for unsafe example risk. The permission handler denies non-git shell commands but returns approved true for other request kinds. This is not malicious, but production integrations should not broadly approve unknown permission requests.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example clearly returns approval for requests outside the shell branch. It is documentation rather than active code, so the risk depends on users copying it into a real integration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The many Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown inline code spans, code fences, package names, commands, type names, and examples in SKILL.md. The skill package contains documentation, not executable Ruby backtick expressions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visibly Markdown bullets, table cells, and fenced code examples. No Ruby source file or executable skill script is present in the static results.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak-cryptography alerts match ordinary words, model names, table headers, or method names in Markdown documentation. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic implementation is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed cited areas are prose, table rows, and troubleshooting text. I found no evidence of cryptographic code or weak algorithm use.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious networking. The URLs point to GitHub Copilot MCP, an example Azure OpenAI endpoint, GitHub documentation, GitHub source, and the MCP specification. No code sends local data or credentials to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are documented integration endpoints and references. I did not find evidence of exfiltration logic or hidden network behavior.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign SDK Operations
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The system reconnaissance alert maps to a session prompt asking what was discussed, and the network reconnaissance alert maps to starting a Copilot CLI server on a local port. These are normal examples for session resume and external CLI connection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines do not enumerate hosts, scan networks, or collect system information. They show SDK session continuation and a local CLI port example.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Local Command Execution Surface
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes install commands, a local MCP server command, and a Copilot headless CLI command. These are examples rather than active skill behavior, but they should be treated as command-execution guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly present commands or command configuration. The risk is contextual because the Markdown skill does not execute them itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Secret Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents GitHub token and BYOK API key inputs through environment variables. This is normal for SDK setup, but it requires secure secret handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential variables and API key configuration are explicit. There is no evidence that the skill reads or transmits secrets by itself.

content.value_statement: Developers need clear patterns for embedding GitHub Copilot into applications and agent workflows. This skill provides concise SDK examples for sessions, tools, hooks, MCP servers, streaming, and provider configuration.
actual_capabilities: Shows basic Copilot SDK session creation and message sending across four programming languages.
Explains streaming response handlers and event subscription patterns.
Documents custom tool definitions, hooks, and permission control examples.
Covers remote and local MCP server configuration for Copilot sessions.
Describes authentication options, BYOK provider setup, session persistence, and custom agents.
limitations: Requires a working GitHub Copilot CLI installation and authentication for standard Copilot usage.
Provides documentation examples only; it does not include runnable tests or helper scripts.
Examples use placeholder paths, session IDs, endpoints, and environment variables that users must adapt.
Local MCP and permission examples require careful review before use in production environments.

use_cases.title: Prototype Copilot-powered tools
use_cases.description: Use the session and message examples to build a first Copilot SDK integration quickly.

use_cases.title: Add controlled agent tools
use_cases.description: Use the custom tool and hook patterns to expose application functions with permission checks.

use_cases.title: Plan enterprise provider setups
use_cases.description: Review BYOK, MCP, and persistence examples before designing an internal Copilot workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Create a first session
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the simplest Copilot SDK session flow for my preferred language, including startup, one prompt, and shutdown.

prompt_templates.title: Add streaming output
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt my Copilot SDK session to stream assistant output and explain the event handlers I need.

prompt_templates.title: Design a guarded tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define a Copilot SDK custom tool with clear parameters, validation, and permission checks.

prompt_templates.title: Architect an MCP BYOK workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Copilot SDK architecture that uses MCP servers, BYOK provider settings, session persistence, and safe permission handling.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to start a Copilot SDK session in TypeScript.
output_examples.output: A short setup plan with package installation, client creation, session creation, a test prompt, and cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: A platform engineer asks how to control tool execution.
output_examples.output: A hook-based permission design that allows trusted operations, denies shell access by default, and records decision reasons.

output_examples.input: An AI lead asks how BYOK affects persistence.
output_examples.output: A design note explaining provider configuration, session resume requirements, and key handling boundaries.

best_practices: Keep tokens and provider keys in approved secret storage and pass them through environment variables only when needed.
Restrict MCP server tools, working directories, and command arguments before enabling local integrations.
Use hooks to review tool calls, permission requests, and user input before actions run.
anti_patterns: Do not auto-approve broad tool access in production sessions.
Do not hardcode real GitHub tokens, API keys, or provider credentials in source files.
Do not expose filesystem MCP servers to unrestricted paths or unreviewed tool lists.

faq.question: Does this skill install the Copilot SDK?
faq.answer: No. It documents installation commands and integration patterns that users run in their own projects.

faq.question: Which languages are covered?
faq.answer: The skill includes examples for Node.js and TypeScript, Python, Go, and .NET.

faq.question: Does standard usage require a Copilot subscription?
faq.answer: Yes. Standard Copilot SDK usage requires GitHub Copilot CLI authentication and an active subscription.

faq.question: Can it use non-Copilot model providers?
faq.answer: Yes. The BYOK section describes provider configuration for OpenAI-compatible, Azure, Anthropic, and local endpoints.

faq.question: Is this safe for production use as written?
faq.answer: The examples need production review, especially MCP servers, permission handlers, local commands, and credential handling.

faq.question: Does the skill contain executable helper scripts?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed package contains a Markdown skill file with documentation and code examples.
